summary: The author developed a chess engine evaluation function for a Kaggle competition, focusing on a binary-size-efficient NNUE architecture. They replaced the standard feature transformer with a hybrid approach combining a CNN for per-square features and a weight-shared dense network to drastically reduce input dimensionality. Despite the architectural size savings, they discovered the hybrid design incurred a heavy computational cost due to a large equivalent L1 size, which negated its accuracy benefits. The network was trained on a mix of Leela and Stockfish-generated data, and the surrounding engine was optimized with specific memory allocations and compiler flags.
extensive_summary:
## NNUE Architecture
The core of the solution revolves around designing a binary-size-efficient NNUE evaluation function. The authors disabled HCE and relied entirely on NNUE to prioritize evaluation accuracy. To minimize network size, they replaced the standard feature transformer with a hybrid approach that concatenates outputs from two sub-networks. The first is a CNN with 12 input channels (representing the pieces), 13 output channels, a 15x15 kernel, and 7 padding, designed to generate features for each square. The second is a dense network with 64 outputs that shares weights across neighbor positions, reducing the input from 768 to just 96.

## Training Data & Computational Trade-offs
The network was trained on a mixture of Leela-generated and Stockfish-generated data, though the authors noted that swapping the dataset source had minimal impact on performance. A critical realization was that the hybrid architecture incurred a heavy computational cost. The equivalent L1 size reached 896, which was too large to justify the accuracy loss introduced by the weight-sharing in the CNN.

## Engine Configuration & Compilation
Beyond the network, the authors optimized the surrounding chess engine environment. They configured the transposition table to 512 KiB and used a 128 KiB hash table for continuation history. Compilation was split for performance: `nnue.c` was compiled with `-O3` optimization, while all other files used `-Os`. They also pruned unused features that did not significantly impact performance.
competition_flow: The pipeline involved designing a custom hybrid CNN-dense NNUE architecture for chess position evaluation, training it on a mix of Leela and Stockfish game data, integrating it into a modified chess engine with optimized memory and compilation flags, and submitting the resulting engine configuration.
insights: Relying entirely on NNUE while disabling HCE was a deliberate choice to prioritize evaluation accuracy.
Replacing the standard feature transformer with a hybrid CNN and weight-shared dense network successfully reduced the input dimensionality from 768 to 96.
The computational cost of the hybrid architecture (equivalent L1 size of 896) ultimately outweighed the accuracy benefits of the shared weights.
The choice of training data source (Leela vs. Stockfish) had minimal impact on the final network performance.
critical_findings: The hybrid CNN-dense architecture, while effective at shrinking input size, introduced a heavy computational bottleneck due to its large equivalent L1 size.
Swapping between Leela-generated and Stockfish-generated training datasets produced negligible performance differences.
what_did_not_work: The hybrid CNN-dense feature transformer approach was abandoned because its equivalent L1 size (896) was too large to compensate for the accuracy loss from shared weights.
